

# B21_KEY_MOMENTS

## Key Quotes

**"I submit to you that you do not have a technology problem. You don't have a tactics problem. You have a mental model problem."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Opening framing after presenting survey data showing 80% of respondents felt stuck despite using ChatGPT. Reframed the entire session from "learn these tools" to "change how you think about AI" — the thesis of the workshop.

**"It's not that you lack the tools or even the knowledge — it's that it feels like you're collecting individual techniques without actually understanding how they fit."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Diagnosing why people feel overwhelmed. Identifies the core failure mode: technique accumulation without a binding mental model. Sets up the three-level framework (conversation, environment, pipeline).

**"When I say AI is magic, I essentially mean that we are reducing the gap between intent and outcome."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Defusing the "tech bro hype" framing by redefining magic as collapsed cognitive distance — the DoorDash analogy (burrito taxi vs. growing food). Grounds an abstract concept in everyday experience.

**"You don't prompt the AI per se. You work with the AI to cultivate the context that it then uses to collapse down to the output that you want."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* The single most important mental model shift in the presentation. Moves the audience from "prompting" to "context engineering" — from writing instructions to priming an environment.

**"LinkedIn has everything that you have ever done, but they only show you 10% of it."**
— Logan Currie
*Context:* Framing the LinkedIn data export experiment. Reveals a hidden asymmetry — platforms collect vastly more than they surface — and positions personal data analysis as a way to reclaim that visibility.

**"You have 23 high-value DMs from the last six months."**
— Logan Currie (quoting Claude's analysis)
*Context:* The moment the LinkedIn data experiment went from "cool visualization" to "genuinely useful." Claude surfaced ghosted collaboration opportunities and podcast invitations that LinkedIn's UI buried. Concrete ROI from personal data + AI.

**"Job security is dead and you need to be building career security for yourself."**
— Logan Currie
*Context:* Logan's overarching thesis, connecting the data experiments back to professional strategy. Frames visibility and findability as career infrastructure, not vanity metrics.

**"You can unhobble your AI a lot when you give it access to your files and data. And you can unhobble it even more when you give the AI its own home."**
— Ben Guo
*Context:* Building on Anthropic's "unhobbling" concept to explain why giving AI persistent compute (a computer, not just a chat window) is a step change. The philosophical foundation for Zo Computer's product thesis.

**"I think this is the future of coding — just planning out the system in this kind of nice mix of prose and code in these stepwise fashions."**
— Ben Guo
*Context:* Showing his plan files in the Zo codebase. Signals a shift from "writing code" to "planning systems that generate code" — relevant to the non-technical audience as a preview of where software creation is heading.

## Strategic Questions Raised

**"What's LinkedIn sitting on that they should be showing us?"**
— Logan Currie
*Significance:* The prompt that unlocked the most surprising insights from Claude. Demonstrates the "unhobbling" principle in practice — asking the AI to think beyond what you'd think to ask. A reusable meta-prompt pattern for any personal data analysis.

**"Come up with two to three things that would really delight me."**
— Logan Currie (prompt to Claude)
*Significance:* Models the "delight me" prompting pattern — explicitly asking the AI to exceed expectations rather than just answer the question. Produced the DM analysis and connection timeline visualizations that weren't in Logan's original plan.

**"Why do I have more imported contacts than connections?"**
— Logan Currie (live discovery during the session)
*Significance:* A real-time moment of discovery that revealed LinkedIn's 2015 mass contact import created a shadow network of people Logan never connected with. Demonstrated the value of exploring data live rather than presenting polished results — authenticity as pedagogy.
